annotations.label: Ratio

anchor.snippet: a ratio

annotations.note: Mathlib has no abstract `Ratio` notion; ratios are handled as ordinary division/fractions in a field.

annotations.label: Oranges to lemons ratio

anchor.snippet: if there are eight oranges and six lemons in a bowl of fruit

annotations.note: Numerical illustration, not a theorem; not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proportion

anchor.snippet: A statement expressing the equality of two ratios is called a proportion

annotations.note: Equality of ratios is just an equality of quotients in Mathlib; no named `Proportion` predicate.

annotations.label: Ratio in physical sciences (dimensionless quotient)

anchor.snippet: A more specific definition adopted in physical sciences

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dimensional-analysis framework that distinguishes dimensionless quotients.

annotations.label: Rate

anchor.snippet: A quotient of two quantities that are measured with different units may be called a rate

annotations.note: No units/rate framework in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Terms, antecedent, consequent

anchor.snippet: The numbers A and B are sometimes called terms of the ratio

annotations.note: Terminology only; not a Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Proportion and its terms

anchor.snippet: A statement expressing the equality of two ratios A : B and C : D is called a proportion

annotations.note: Not formalized as a named structure in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extremes and means

anchor.snippet: A and D are called its extremes , and B and C are called its means

annotations.note: Classical terminology with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Continued proportion

anchor.snippet: is called a continued proportion

annotations.note: No `ContinuedProportion` concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-by-four edge length proportion

anchor.snippet: the proportion for the edge lengths of a " two by four " that is ten inches long

annotations.note: Numerical example, not a Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Concrete mix ratio

anchor.snippet: A good concrete mix (in volume units) is sometimes quoted as

annotations.note: Applied example; not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Part and multiple of a quantity

anchor.snippet: The first two definitions say that a part of a quantity is another quantity that "measures" it

annotations.note: Euclid's 'measures' corresponds informally to divisibility, formalized as `Dvd.dvd` in Lean core, but no Euclid-specific definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclid's general definition of ratio (Def. 3)

anchor.snippet: Definition 3 describes what a ratio is in a general way

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Euclid's informal Definition 3.

annotations.label: Existence of a ratio / Archimedean property (Def. 4)

anchor.snippet: Definition 4 makes this more rigorous

annotations.note: The Archimedean property is formalized as the typeclass `Archimedean`.

annotations.label: Equality of ratios (Def. 5)

anchor.snippet: Definition 5 is the most complex and difficult

annotations.note: Eudoxus's cross-multiplication criterion is not formalized as a distinct Mathlib notion.

annotations.label: Proportional quantities (Def. 6)

anchor.snippet: Definition 6 says that quantities that have the same ratio are proportional or in proportion

annotations.note: No `Proportional` definition in Mathlib matching Euclid Def. 6.

annotations.label: Comparison of ratios (Def. 7)

anchor.snippet: Definition 7 defines what it means for one ratio to be less than or greater than another

annotations.note: Euclidean ratio comparison is not a separately named concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three or more terms in proportion; geometric progressions (Def. 8)

anchor.snippet: definition 8 is regarded by some as being a later insertion

annotations.note: Geometric progressions exist in Mathlib (e.g., `geom_series`, `geom_sum_eq`), but Euclid's Definition 8 itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Duplicate and triplicate ratios (Defs. 9 and 10)

anchor.snippet: Definitions 9 and 10 apply this

annotations.note: No duplicate/triplicate ratio definitions in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Oranges and apples ratio as fractions

anchor.section: Number of terms and use of fractions
anchor.snippet: If there are 2 oranges and 3 apples

annotations.note: Pedagogical example, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ratio with three entities (2:3:7)

anchor.section: Number of terms and use of fractions
anchor.snippet: from a ratio of 2:3:7 we can infer

annotations.note: Numerical example, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invariance of ratio under common multiplication

anchor.snippet: If we multiply all quantities involved in a ratio by the same number, the ratio remains valid

annotations.note: Equivalent to `mul_div_mul_left : (c*a)/(c*b) = a/b`, an algebraic fact in Mathlib, though not stated as a 'ratio invariance' proposition.

annotations.label: Mixture A:B:C:D = 5:9:4:2

anchor.snippet: If a mixture contains substances A , B , C and D in the ratio 5:9:4:2

annotations.note: Numerical example, not formalized.

annotations.label: Proportion as comparison to the whole

anchor.snippet: This comparison of a specific quantity to "the whole" is called a proportion

annotations.note: No 'part-to-whole proportion' definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Aspect ratios of televisions

anchor.snippet: older televisions have a 4:3 aspect ratio

annotations.note: Applied example; not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction of ratios

anchor.snippet: Ratios can be reduced (as fractions are) by dividing each quantity by the common factors

annotations.note: Rational numbers are stored in reduced form via `Rat`, but ratio-reduction as a named operation is not a separate Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Simplest form / lowest terms

anchor.snippet: A ratio that has integers for both quantities and that cannot be reduced any further

annotations.note: Lowest terms corresponds to coprimality of numerator and denominator (`Nat.Coprime`); `Rat` enforces this invariant.

annotations.label: Writing 4:5 in the form 1:x or x:1

anchor.snippet: the ratio 4:5 can be written as 1:1.25

annotations.note: Arithmetic example, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Irrational (incommensurable) ratios

anchor.snippet: Ratios may also be established between incommensurable quantities

annotations.note: Incommensurable ratio = irrational real; formalized as `Irrational : ℝ → Prop`.

annotations.label: Diagonal-to-side ratio of a square

anchor.snippet: the ratio of the length of the diagonal d to the length of a side s of a square

annotations.note: The diagonal-to-side ratio √2 is shown irrational by `irrational_sqrt_two`.

annotations.label: Ratio of circumference to diameter (π)

anchor.snippet: Another example is the ratio of a circle 's circumference to its diameter

annotations.note: `Real.pi` is defined and `irrational_pi : Irrational Real.pi` is proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden ratio

anchor.snippet: Also well known is the golden ratio of two (mostly) lengths a and b

annotations.note: `Real.goldenRatio := (1 + √5)/2` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden ratio as limit of Fibonacci ratios

anchor.snippet: An example of an occurrence of the golden ratio in math is as the limiting value

annotations.note: States `fib(n+1)/fib(n) → Real.goldenRatio`.

annotations.label: Silver ratio

anchor.snippet: Similarly, the silver ratio of a and b is defined by the proportion

annotations.note: No `silverRatio` declaration found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Odds as ratios

anchor.snippet: odds of "7 to 3 against" (7:3)

annotations.note: Gambling odds terminology, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unitless ratios

anchor.snippet: Ratios may be unitless , as in the case they relate quantities in units of the same dimension

annotations.note: Mathlib has no units/dimension framework distinguishing unitless ratios.

annotations.label: Rates (non-dimensionless quotients)

anchor.snippet: there are non-dimensionless quotients, also known as rates

annotations.note: No formal notion of dimensioned rates in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Barycentric coordinates

anchor.snippet: In barycentric coordinates , a point with coordinates α, β, γ

annotations.note: Barycentric coordinates relative to an affine basis are formalized as `AffineBasis.coord`.

annotations.label: Trilinear coordinates

anchor.snippet: In trilinear coordinates , a point with coordinates x : y : z

annotations.note: No trilinear-coordinates declaration found in Mathlib.
